title: Chassis Thermal Runaway Risk (Blade Enclosures)
exclusions: Cisco Intersight–primary domains where you intentionally manage thermal posture and policy in Intersight (not the same saved-search contract as a classic **UCS Manager** `cisco:ucs:stats` poll through **Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs**); Cisco HyperFlex HX environments where cooling and host telemetry follow the HX data model, not the chassis MO mix described here; C-series servers in standalone **CIMC**-only mode or rack scale-out that is not represented under `sys/chassis-*` in the UCSM tree you poll; **UCS Director** or other aggregators that reshape statistics into a non-UCSM schema; and any estate where the authoritative thermal contract is a facilities BMS or row sensor feed rather than UCSM stats — use those UCs, not a duplicate chassis runaway detector. If you want the steady-state kW/thermal **threshold board** that compares the latest sample per `dn` to `ucs_environmental_thresholds.csv`, that is **UC-19.1.6**; this use case is the **predictive runaway** detector, so do not file both as the same runbook without naming the difference.
dataSources: `index=cisco_ucs` (or your org-standard index) with sourcetype **`cisco:ucs:stats`** from the Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (**Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs**, Splunkbase 2731). The statistics **modular input** polls **UCS Manager** over HTTPS and emits one stream per managed-object class; this use case ingests a blended set of `classId` values — **`equipmentChassisStats`** (ambient/inlet class chassis thermal path, plus `inputPower` when you extend the math), **`equipmentFanStats`** and **`equipmentFanModuleStats`** (per-fan and module fan **RPM** and `operState`), **`processorEnvStats`** (per-socket CPU temperature as a faster leading indicator), **`computeMbTempStats`** (motherboard/thermistor front/rear/IO paths), and **`equipmentIOCardStats`** (IOM/IO path temperature where present) — and feeds **binned** `_time` series per `chassis_dn` so **`streamstats`** can derive **°C per hour** from consecutive 5-minute buckets, correlate against **fan** drop or **fan_failed**, and support **linear extrapolation** to minutes-until-inlet-crit from `ucs_chassis_thermal_runaway_thresholds.csv`.
app: `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` ([Splunkbase 2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731), v4.3.x or newer, supported on Splunk Enterprise 9.x, 10.x, and Splunk Cloud). The on-disk directory name is underscore-delimited; package filenames with hyphens are packaging only.
spl:
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:stats" earliest=-90m@m
| eval cidl=lower(coalesce(classId, class_id, ""))
| where cidl IN ("equipmentchassisstats","equipmentfanstats","equipmentfanmodulestats","processorenvstats","computembtempstats","equipmentiocardstats")
| rex field=dn max_match=0 "^sys/chassis-(?<chassis_num>\d+)"
| where isnotnull(chassis_num)
| eval chassis_dn="sys/chassis-".chassis_num
| eval temp_inlet=if(cidl="equipmentchassisstats", tonumber(coalesce(ambientTemp, ambient_temp, ioTemp, io_temp, frontTemp, front_temp, rearTemp, rear_temp, temp, temperature, ambient, ambientC)), null())
| eval temp_cpu=if(cidl="processorenvstats", tonumber(coalesce(temperature, temp)), null())
| eval temp_mb=if(cidl="computembtempstats", tonumber(coalesce(temperature, temp, tmrFront, tmr_front, tmrRear, tmr_rear, tmrIO, tmr_io)), null())
| eval iom_t=if(cidl="equipmentiocardstats", tonumber(coalesce(temperature, temp, ambientEnvTemp, ambient_env_temp, envTemp, env_temp)), null())
| eval fan_rpm=if(cidl IN ("equipmentfanstats","equipmentfanmodulestats"), tonumber(coalesce(speed, fan_speed, rpm, fanRpm, fan_rpm, measuredSpeed, measured_speed)), null())
| eval fan_oper=if(cidl IN ("equipmentfanstats","equipmentfanmodulestats"), lower(coalesce(operState, oper_state, "")), null())
| eval fan_failed=if(isnotnull(fan_oper) AND fan_oper!="operable" AND fan_oper!="" AND fan_oper!="ok", 1, 0)
| bin _time span=5m
| stats avg(temp_inlet) as bin_temp max(temp_cpu) as bin_cpu_max max(temp_mb) as bin_mb_max max(iom_t) as bin_iom_max avg(fan_rpm) as bin_fan_rpm max(fan_failed) as bin_fan_failed by chassis_dn, chassis_num, _time
| sort 0 chassis_dn, _time
| streamstats current=f last(bin_temp) as prev_temp, last(bin_fan_rpm) as prev_fan_rpm, last(_time) as prev_time by chassis_dn
| eval bin_seconds=_time-prev_time
| eval rate_c_per_hour=if(isnull(prev_temp) OR isnull(bin_temp) OR isnull(bin_seconds) OR bin_seconds=0, null(), round((bin_temp - prev_temp) / bin_seconds * 3600, 2))
| eval fan_drop_pct=if(isnull(prev_fan_rpm) OR isnull(bin_fan_rpm) OR prev_fan_rpm=0, null(), round((prev_fan_rpm - bin_fan_rpm) / prev_fan_rpm * 100, 1))
| sort 0 chassis_dn, -_time
| dedup chassis_dn
| lookup ucs_chassis_model_map chassis_num OUTPUT chassis_model
| fillnull value="UCSB-5108" chassis_model
| eval sensor_class="inlet_ambient"
| lookup ucs_chassis_thermal_runaway_thresholds chassis_model sensor_class OUTPUT crit_temp_c, default_fan_rpm
| fillnull value=35 crit_temp_c
| fillnull value=10000 default_fan_rpm
| eval mins_to_crit=if(isnotnull(rate_c_per_hour) AND rate_c_per_hour>0 AND isnotnull(bin_temp) AND bin_temp<crit_temp_c, round((crit_temp_c - bin_temp) / rate_c_per_hour * 60, 0), null())
| eval cooling_status=case(bin_fan_failed=1, "fan_failed", isnotnull(fan_drop_pct) AND fan_drop_pct>=20, "fan_dropping_20pct", isnotnull(fan_drop_pct) AND fan_drop_pct>=15, "fan_dropping_15pct", 1=1, "ok")
| eval severity_bucket=case(isnotnull(rate_c_per_hour) AND rate_c_per_hour>=5 AND (cooling_status="fan_failed" OR cooling_status="fan_dropping_20pct") AND isnotnull(mins_to_crit) AND mins_to_crit<=30, "critical", (isnotnull(rate_c_per_hour) AND rate_c_per_hour>=3) OR (isnotnull(fan_drop_pct) AND fan_drop_pct>=15) OR (isnotnull(mins_to_crit) AND mins_to_crit<=60), "warning", isnotnull(rate_c_per_hour) AND rate_c_per_hour>=1.5, "watch", 1=1, "ok")
| eval sev_order=case(severity_bucket="critical",1, severity_bucket="warning",2, severity_bucket="watch",3, 1=1,4)
| table chassis_dn, chassis_num, bin_temp, bin_fan_rpm, bin_cpu_max, bin_mb_max, bin_iom_max, rate_c_per_hour, fan_drop_pct, cooling_status, mins_to_crit, severity_bucket, sev_order
| sort 0 sev_order, chassis_dn
description: Consumes `cisco:ucs:stats` for `classId` in `equipmentChassisStats`, `equipmentFanStats`, `equipmentFanModuleStats`, `processorEnvStats`, `computeMbTempStats`, and `equipmentIOCardStats`; derives `chassis_dn` and `chassis_num` from `dn` with `^sys/chassis-(?<chassis_num>\d+)`; bins `_time` to 5m and applies **`streamstats`** so each chassis gets **rate-of-rise** in **°C/hour** from successive inlet (`equipmentChassisStats` via **`coalesce(ambientTemp,…)`** ) bucket means; pairs that trend with **fan** RPM drop (percent change between buckets) and **`fan_failed`** from non-operable `operState`; uses **`lookup`**ed per-model `crit_temp_c` to produce **minutes to inlet critical** by linear extrapolation `(crit - current) / rate * 60` when rate is positive; emits **`severity_bucket`** (critical when rapid rise, strong cooling loss signal, and ≤30 min to crit). This is a predictive runaway signature detector, not a static threshold board. If you want the steady-state thermal and kW threshold board, that is **UC-19.1.6**; this use case is the predictive runaway detector — **rate of rise** plus **cooling-failure correlation** plus **time-to-shutdown extrapolation** — a chassis that is fine on **UC-19.1.6** right now can still be in runaway according to this use case.
value: A blade enclosure can go from unremarkable inlet readings to a multi-blade thermal event in minutes when cold air thins, a fan or controller misbehaves, or a cooling path degrades, and guest workloads then see throttle and brownouts together. Catching a sustained degree-per-hour climb while fan speed falls or a fan is non-operable gives facilities and the NOC a time-to-crit estimate instead of a late alarm at a static red line, which reduces the chance of a coordinated shutdown across neighbors and tenant VMs that only show pain after hardware has already pulled margin away. The business outcome is a longer lead time to re-home or drain hosts, to open a controlled change or an RMA before SLA clocks start on the application tier, and to avoid a surprise corridor event where a rack’s density turns a local cooling problem into a multi-customer service story.
implementation: Install **Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs** on a Heavy Forwarder and Search Heads, enable the UCS **statistics** modular input to your **UCSM** VIP with index `cisco_ucs` (or your standard), confirm `classId` coverage, deploy `ucs_chassis_model_map.csv` and `ucs_chassis_thermal_runaway_thresholds.csv` with `transforms.conf`, then save the `spl` as a 5m test panel and, after two poll cycles, schedule a throttled **alert** on `severity_bucket` in `critical` and `warning` with a facilities hand-off field list.
visualization: (1) **Heatmap** of `chassis_num` (or a short `chassis_dn` label) × `_time` with cell colour from `bin_temp` or from `rate_c_per_hour` to show spatially which enclosures are **heating** fastest. (2) **Line chart** of **inlet** `bin_temp` with a second series (dotted) for **fan** `bin_fan_rpm` re-added from a clone search or joined post-processing so operators see **divergence** (temperature up while **RPM** falls) that defines the runaway **signature** before a static threshold trips. (3) **Single value** count of `chassis_dn` rows where `severity_bucket` is `critical` (and a second tile for `warning`). (4) **Table** of `chassis_dn`, `bin_temp`, `rate_c_per_hour`, `fan_drop_pct`, `cooling_status`, **`mins_to_crit`**, and `severity_bucket` sorted with **critical** first — this is the paging row set for the NOC.

references.title: Cisco UCS 5108 Blade Server Chassis installation and service notes (mechanical, airflow, and cooling context)
ASHRAE — Thermal Guidelines for Data Processing Environments (data center class and operating ranges)
Splunk — streamstats command reference (windowed per-chassis rate math)

knownFalsePositives:
• (a) **HVAC** zone or **CRAH** / **CRAC** handover, economizer cut-over, or staged **setpoint** moves can lift inlet a few degrees for several bins without a hardware fault; expect co-present facilities tickets and stable **fan** **RPM** rather than a **fan_failed** state.
• (b) Open hot- or cold-aisle containment doors during a data-center tour, shipping dock weather intrusion, or short-term fire-panel drill airflow changes — transient inlet slope without sustained **fan** derate.
• (c) **Blade** or **IOM** **firmware** **activation** with a brief **CPU** socket **temperature** lift on **`processorEnvStats`** while **inlet** mean remains stable; the primary **runaway** gate on **`equipmentChassisStats`** should not page alone without **fan** trend agreement.
• (d) **PID**-style **fan** **controller** **overshoot** after a large workload step-down: **RPM** can dip then recover while **inlet** is still moving; require two consecutive 5m bins in production paging, or a short dedupe on **`chassis_dn`**, before **RMA** **hardware** **fans**.
• (e) **MO** or service-processor **clock skew** between **UCSM** poll, forwarder, and `_time` bucketing: apparent **°C/hour** spikes when bins misalign; compare **`_indextime` − `_time`** and **NTP** on the management path if **Sev1** **trust** is questioned.
• (f) A single-poll **sensor** **glitch** or null-filled sample that collapses a bin mean; the **`dedup` after time sort** is intentionally **latest**-row focused — keep a companion **`timechart` `count` by `chassis_dn`** to detect sparse feeds.
• (g) **Calibration** **drift** on long-uptime **thermistor** paths vs **DIMM**-adjacent **motherboard** sensors: slow crawl that is real but not an emergency; track **`computeMbTempStats`** against **`equipmentChassisStats`** in a secondary panel, not the same static crit as **inlet**.
• (h) Planned **chassis** or **IOM** **maintenance** with known **suppression** — pair alerts with a change id so a deliberate fan profile change does not equal **runaway**.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Sibling use case, explicit scope: **UC-19.1.6** is the steady-state kW, fan, and thermal threshold board: latest-24h sample per `dn` vs `ucs_environmental_thresholds.csv` for OK/Warn/Critical/NoBaseline. This use case, **UC-19.1.34**, is the predictive thermal runaway path: binned °C/hour from `cisco:ucs:stats`, fan RPM delta and `fan_failed`, and **linear extrapolation** to minutes until inlet critical from `ucs_chassis_thermal_runaway_thresholds.csv`. If you want the threshold board, implement **UC-19.1.6**; if you want the “are we about to hit shutdown in tens of minutes?” pager, implement 19.1.34. A chassis that is fine on UC-19.1.6 right now can still be in runaway according to this use case. Do not merge the two into one KPI without naming the difference in your NOC runbook header.
• Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, Splunkbase 2731) on a Heavy Forwarder (or any splunkd host permitted to run the Cisco UCS statistics modular input) and on Search Heads for field discovery, lookup files, and alerts.
• A UCSM read-only or least-privilege API user whose entitlements include statistics MOs in the `equipment*` and `processor*` classes used by this search, TLS trust to the UCSM VIP or cluster name, and outbound 443/tcp from the collection host.
• An approved `index`, usually `cisco_ucs`, in `srchIndexesAllowed` for operators who run or own the saved search. Indexers and SH should share the same TA props for consistent parsing.
• Baseline: know your aisle design (18–22C cold-aisle vs warmer ASHRAE-aligned supply) so `crit_temp_c` in the CSV is not copied from a blog post; edit the lookup in week one.
• Documentation warning you must print in your internal wiki when you retire bad examples: some internal wikis or older runbooks may say `cisco:ucs:environmental` — that is a documentation bug; the canonical **thermal** statistics **sourcetype** is `cisco:ucs:stats` with `classId in (**equipmentChassisStats**, equipmentFanStats, equipmentFanModuleStats, processorEnvStats, computeMbTempStats, equipmentIOCardStats)` (extend with `equipmentPsuStats` or `computeMbPowerStats` only if you add power-correlated logic; not required for the inlet runaway signature in the `spl` field).

**Step 1 — Configure data collection and wire inputs.conf**
(1) Install `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` on the HF and Search Heads per vendor notes for your Splunk platform version.
(2) In **Settings** → **Data inputs** → the Cisco UCS statistics input (wording varies slightly by add-on build), set host, port 443, index to your standard (`cisco_ucs` here), HTTPS with full trust chain, and enable statistics classes broad enough to include the `classId` list in the `dataSources` field. After two polls, `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:stats" earliest=-1h | stats count by classId` must list the classes you enabled.
(3) File-driven alternative with a literal `inputs.conf` block you can paste into `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/local/inputs.conf` (keys must match the stanza your build ships — names differ slightly; treat this as a pattern, not a brand-new stanza name):

```ini
# Pattern — copy the VENDOR stanza from default/inputs.conf, then local override:
# [cisco_ucs://<a_vendor_stanza_name>]
# disabled = 0
# index = cisco_ucs
# start_by_shell = 1
# (Set URL / hostname / username / password or secrets handler per your site.)
# Leave sourcetype assignment to the vendor defaults for the statistics stream so events remain cisco:ucs:stats.
```

(4) Restart only as required, then on the HF `tail` `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/splunkd.log` for successful UCSM HTTPS and XML fetch without 401/403/SSL errors.
(5) Volume planning: more enabled MO classes and shorter interval raise license cost; the `spl` field assumes 5m bins, which tolerates 3–5 minute polls on most sites.

**Step 2 — Install lookups and `transforms.conf`, then validate `**streamstats**` rate math**
(1) On the Search Head app that owns your UCS dashboards (example `cisco_ucs_ops`), add two CSV files and matching stanzas so `| inputlookup` works for your RBAC role before you save the main search.
(2) Literal `transforms.conf` (merge into the same app’s `local/`):

```ini
[ucs_chassis_model_map]
filename = ucs_chassis_model_map.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
min_matches = 1
default_match =
max_matches = 1

[ucs_chassis_thermal_runaway_thresholds]
filename = ucs_chassis_thermal_runaway_thresholds.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
min_matches = 1
default_match =
max_matches = 1
```

(3) Literal `ucs_chassis_model_map.csv` (seed rows — replace and extend with CMDB truth; chassis numbers must match the `chassis_num` your `dn` rex extracts):

```csv
chassis_num,chassis_model,notes
1,UCSB-5108,EXAMPLE only — map each sys/chassis-N to the physical model in UCSM/CMDB
2,UCSX-9508,EXAMPLE only — X9508 is denser; keep per-site accuracy
```

(4) Literal `ucs_chassis_thermal_runaway_thresholds.csv`, `sensor_class= inlet_ambient` for `equipmentChassisStats`–driven `bin_temp`, with planning `crit_temp_c` values you tune with your mechanical team (example 35 for UCSB-5108-class inlet headroom, 33 for UCSX-9508-class high density — not warranty statements):

```csv
chassis_model,sensor_class,crit_temp_c,default_fan_rpm,notes
UCSB-5108,inlet_ambient,35,10000,5108 B-series: planning inlet; edit per hall and ASHRAE class
UCSX-9508,inlet_ambient,33,10000,X9508: earlier inlet headroom — tune with your CFD / facilities owner
```

(5) `metadata/local.meta`: if your org replicates lookups to indexers, export the CSV or keep search-time only on SH; either is acceptable if `lookup` resolves in Search.
(6) Rate-of-rise example as a teaching snippet (run on a single chassis to see math before you dedup fleet-wide). This shows the core of `streamstats`: sort by time ascending per chassis, carry `last(...)` from the previous row, and convert delta-over-seconds to °C per hour with `* 3600 / seconds` (equivalent to `(curr-prev)/(t_curr-t_prev)*3600` when `**bin_seconds**` is non-zero):

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:stats" earliest=-2h classId="equipmentChassisStats"
| rex field=dn max_match=0 "^sys/chassis-(?<chassis_num>\d+)"
| where chassis_num="1"
| bin _time span=5m
| stats avg(tonumber(coalesce(ambientTemp, ambient_temp, frontTemp, front_temp, rearTemp, rear_temp))) as bin_temp by _time
| sort 0 _time
| streamstats current=f last(bin_temp) as prev_temp, last(_time) as prev_time
| eval bin_seconds=_time-prev_time
| eval rate_c_per_hour=if(isnull(prev_temp) OR isnull(bin_temp) OR bin_seconds=0, null(), (bin_temp-prev_temp)/bin_seconds*3600)
| table _time, bin_temp, prev_temp, rate_c_per_hour
```

(7) Copy the full `spl` field of this use case as your saved search after the teaching run proves your `chassis_num` and classId coverage.

**Step 3 — Validate in UCSM and against NTP**
(1) Pick a `chassis_dn` that appears in the table and open the same `sys/chassis-…` object in the UCSM **Equipment** tree; compare magnitude and trend with tolerance for poll skew.
(2) In Search, `| fieldsummary ambientTemp classId` on `equipmentChassisStats`; extend `**coalesce**()` lists in the `spl` field if your API build exposes e.g. `rearTemp` only in your row of the world.
(3) If `fan_rpm` is null but `classId=equipmentFanStats` exists, sample `speed` and `operState` with `| head` and expand `coalesce()` for `fan` fields the same way UC-19.1.6 does for cross-version spelling.
(4) Clocks: if `_indextime` − `_time` is often >300s, treat `rate_c_per_hour` as unreliable until **NTP** on UCSM, service processors, and Splunk infrastructure are clean.

**Step 4 — Operationalize dashboards and alerts**
(1) Build a Predictive thermal runaway dashboard with the four panels described in the `visualization` field: a heatmap for enclosure by time, a line chart of inlet versus dotted fan RPM to show divergence, a single value for Critical, and a table with `mins_to_crit`.
(2) Schedule the saved search on a five-minute cron with `earliest=-90m@m` (or a window you choose) and throttle on `chassis_dn`. Send `severity_bucket=critical` to your paging path; keep `watch` in chat at first so you can tune for HVAC transients before waking people.
(3) Add a second panel row with UC-19.1.6 on the same dashboard so a bridge does not treat a green threshold-board row as proof that 19.1.34 can be ignored.

**Step 5 — Tuning, sibling boundaries, and troubleshooting**
• If `cisco:ucs:stats` is empty — input not running, index name mismatch, or role not allowed to read the index; fix before SPL surgery.
• If `chassis_num` is null for IOM-only or FI-only `dn` paths, those rows are out of scope for the `^sys/chassis-` filter; that is expected — do not declare the index empty when only upstream paths exist.
• If every chassis maps to the `fillnull` default UCSB-5108 model, numeric defaults for `crit_temp_c` may still look plausible but extrapolation is wrong for X9508; refresh `ucs_chassis_model_map` weekly with CMDB or UCSM model truth.
• If warnings flood after a facilities setpoint change, add a time-of-day `where` for known BMS window labels or widen `span=10m` if polls are sparse.
• If you need `equipmentPsuStats` or `equipmentNetworkElementFanStats` on the same board, fork a companion search: mixing PSU kW with inlet rate-of-rise without a power-budget model is a different story than the runaway signature on `equipmentChassisStats` and fan RPM here.

Closing: in a live bridge, say whether a row is from UC-19.1.6 (latest sample versus a policy line) or from **UC-19.1.34** (°C per hour, cooling loss, and minutes to a planning line). A chassis can be acceptable on 19.1.6 and still be in runaway on 19.1.34; both views are valid, and neither dismisses a facilities ticket when only room air moved without a part fault.\n\nDesign note: the linear mins_to_crit math assumes a near-constant **rate of rise** for the next short interval. Real facility events can bend the slope when a UCSM fan policy retargets after load changes, when a CRAC or CRAH handover finishes, or when workload is drained from a chassis. Treat minutes-to-crit as a ranked triage rank, not a physical guarantee, and still pair the alert with a facilities ticket when the room supply air is the more plausible root cause.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the air in front of a shared rack of blade servers. If the temperature is climbing fast and the fans are not keeping up, many machines in that one box can slow down or shut off together. We give a rough idea of how little time is left to move work or get help, not only a bell when a single high line is crossed.
hardware: Cisco UCS 5108, UCS X9508 chassis; examples of fabric-dependent IO modules for airflow context: IOM 2204/2208/2304/2408 on 5108-era designs; UCS X9100-series IFM modules on X9508 when present.
